title: Catching Clouds
passage:
Along the driest coasts on earth, where rain may not fall for years at a stretch, the air still carries water. Sea fog rolls in on most mornings and hangs against the hills until the sun burns it off, and for a few decades now engineers have tried to comb that moisture out of the sky with walls of fine mesh. String the screens across a ridge, the pitch goes, and droplets bead on the fibers, run down into a gutter, and fill a tank below — free water, wrung from a passing cloud. The idea is old, cheap, and almost impossibly appealing, and every few years it returns to the headlines dressed as a cure for the deserts that keep spreading. A recent trial on a fog-belt ridge above the fishing town of Corvane set out to test a narrower claim: that the same screens could keep newly planted trees alive.

The work was led by Dr. Ines Marlow of the Halvard Institute for Arid-Lands Research, whose team put roughly three thousand native thorn-tree seedlings into the ground over three planting seasons, from 2021 through 2023. Some were set behind panels of the mesh; others, the controls, were left in the open. The panels were nothing exotic — double-layered polypropylene netting of the sort used to shade greenhouses, strung between posts a couple of arms wide. On the seaward slope — the flank that meets the morning fog head-on — the difference was hard to miss. After two dry seasons, most of the netted seedlings there were still alive, on the order of two in three, while barely a fifth of the unnetted plants had survived. Marlow's hydrologists reckoned that each panel was delivering a few liters of water a day through the foggiest months, enough, it seemed, to carry a young root across the rainless stretch.

Then the tidy result began to complicate itself. On the leeward slope, tucked behind the crest where the fog thins to a haze, the panels bought almost nothing: the netted and unnetted seedlings died at much the same rate, and the two survival curves ran so close together that Marlow declined to call the gap real. What did keep seedlings alive over there was cruder. A small group on that same dry ground had been watered by hand, a few liters a week hauled in by truck, and roughly seven in ten of them pulled through — better than anything the nets had managed on the fog-poor slope. The water truck broke an axle twice on the switchbacks that first summer, which tells you something about how grudging the ground is. Whatever the screens were doing where they worked, it did not look like a property of the mesh itself. It looked like water, arriving often enough to matter.

Not everyone reads the numbers so cautiously. Rem Kessler, the engineer whose firm designs the mesh arrays, sees a technology on the verge of proving itself. 'People have spent a hundred years telling us these coasts are finished,' he told me, standing beside a panel silvered with beads of dew. 'We can green any dry shore the fog reaches, and the fog reaches a great deal more of the world than anyone has bothered to measure,' he added. It is a stirring pitch, and Kessler may yet be proved partly right; the seaward plots are, after all, real, and they are still standing.

For now, though, the Corvane trial points somewhere more modest than the pitch. The nets earn their keep where the fog is already thick and dependable, and they seem to do it not through any cleverness in the weave but by handing a seedling the one thing the coast withholds — a steady sip of water. Where the fog runs thin, the screens have little to catch, and no width of mesh can conjure moisture from a dry sky. That is a far smaller promise than a cure for the deserts. It may also be the only honest one.

questions.prompt: What is this text mainly about?

options.text: Mesh fog-catchers are a cheap, proven way to reforest almost any arid coastline.
Improved mesh designs have finally made harvesting water from fog affordable at scale.
Fog-nets keep planted trees alive mainly where coastal fog is already reliable.
Planting drought-hardy native trees is the surest defense against spreading deserts.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-004 (main idea), edge position 1. WHY-KEY (C): the whole-text arc runs phenomenon (fog-nets as a cure for the deserts) -> evidence (the netted seedlings thrived on the fog-rich seaward slope) -> complication (on the fog-poor leeward slope the nets bought almost nothing, while hand-watering worked) -> verdict (the nets 'earn their keep where the fog is already thick and dependable' and cannot 'conjure moisture from a dry sky'). C paraphrases that conditional verdict without lifting any 4-word run. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = scope_error, promotes Kessler's broad pitch ('we can green any dry shore the fog reaches') to the passage thesis, which the leeward result explicitly refutes; tempting because it is the passage's most confident single claim. B = surface_word_match, recycles 'mesh'/'fog'/'harvesting water' in an affordability-at-scale claim the text never argues (the pitch was always 'cheap'; nothing was 'finally' made affordable). D = outside_knowledge, a true-sounding desertification truism the passage never asserts (it studies tree survival behind nets, not desert defense in general).

questions.prompt: What are we told about the netted seedlings on the seaward slope?

options.text: About two-thirds of them were still alive after two dry seasons.
Every netted seedling on that slope came through both dry seasons alive.
They died at roughly the same rate as the unnetted plants beside them.
They grew noticeably faster than the seedlings that were watered by hand.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail), local order. WHY-KEY (A): paragraph 2 states that on the seaward slope 'most of the netted seedlings there were still alive, on the order of two in three,' after two dry seasons. A paraphrases 'on the order of two in three' as 'about two-thirds' and keeps the two-dry-season frame. WHY-DISTRACTORS: B = quantifier_upgrade (the dominant engineered trap): the passage's hedged 'most ... on the order of two in three' strengthened to the absolute 'Every ... alive'; tempting as an emphatic reading of a strong result. C = wrong_location, true of the LEEWARD slope ('the netted and unnetted seedlings died at much the same rate') but false on the seaward slope, where the netted plants far outlived the unnetted fifth; tempting to a reader who blurs the two slopes. D = outside_knowledge, a growth-rate comparison against the hand-watered group that the text never makes (it reports survival, not growth speed).

questions.prompt: According to the text, what happened on the leeward slope?

options.text: Because the nets did nothing, none of the seedlings on that slope survived.
The nets made little difference, but a hand-watered group there survived well.
The nets there worked even better than they had on the seaward slope.
Hand-watering was abandoned once trucking the water in proved too costly.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail), local order. WHY-KEY (B): paragraph 3 reports that on the leeward slope 'the panels bought almost nothing' (netted and unnetted died at much the same rate), yet a hand-watered group 'roughly seven in ten of them pulled through.' B paraphrases both halves. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = quantifier_upgrade to an absolute: 'the nets did nothing' overshoots 'bought almost nothing,' and 'none ... survived' is contradicted by the ~70% of the hand-watered group that lived; tempting because the nets' failure is real. C = surface_word_match / reversal, flatly contradicts the text (the nets helped on the seaward slope, not the leeward one); tempting to a reader who inverts the two results. D = outside_knowledge, invents a cost reason for dropping hand-watering that the passage never states; tempting because trucking water in is mentioned.

questions.prompt: What does the trial imply about using the nets on a coast where fog is only occasional?

options.text: Behind the nets, roughly two-thirds of the seedlings would survive the dry seasons.
The nets would still green the coast, since fog reaches almost everywhere.
The seedlings would survive regardless, as thorn trees need almost no water.
The screens would likely help little, with too little fog for them to gather.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-002 (inference), one logical inch beyond the text. WHY-KEY (D): the leeward slope (where 'the fog thins to a haze' and the panels 'bought almost nothing') plus the verdict ('Where the fog runs thin, the screens have little to catch') licenses the one-inch generalization that on an only-occasionally-foggy coast the nets would help little. D states exactly that, no further. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = wrong_transfer (a too-literal carry of the wrong slope's number): it exports the fog-RICH seaward survival figure — 'two in three' — onto a fog-poor coast, the precise error the trial warns against; tempting because two-thirds is the passage's most memorable statistic. B = quantifier_upgrade adopting Kessler's overclaim ('the fog reaches a great deal more of the world' hardened to 'almost everywhere' plus certainty), which the verdict contradicts. C = too_far / outside_knowledge, a two-step leap that thorn trees need almost no water — contradicted by the trial, in which unwatered seedlings largely died.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's attitude toward the idea that the nets could green arid coasts more generally?

options.text: Cautious: drawn to the promise but doubtful it holds beyond genuinely fog-rich sites.
Confident: persuaded that the screens can be scaled to almost any dry shore.
Dismissive: convinced the whole approach is a gimmick not worth pursuing.
Detached: content to report the trial without taking any view of it.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-005 (stance/tone), edge position 5; signature trap is the attribution swap. WHY-KEY (A): the writer grants the appeal ('It is a stirring pitch, and Kessler may yet be proved partly right') but delivers a reserved verdict ('points somewhere more modest than the pitch'; the nets work only 'where the fog is already thick and dependable' and cannot 'conjure moisture from a dry sky'). 'Cautious: drawn to the promise but doubtful it generalizes' names exactly this. WHY-DISTRACTORS: B = role_or_attribution_swap (the signature TYPE-005 trap): it assigns to the writer the conviction of the quoted engineer Kessler, whose line is 'We can green any dry shore the fog reaches'; tempting because that is the passage's most quotable stance. C = polarity_mirror, overshoots the writer's mild reservation into contempt the text never reaches — the writer concedes the nets genuinely work on the seaward slope, so 'gimmick' is too strong. D = tone_misread, mistakes the evaluative verdict ('a far smaller promise ... the only honest one') for neutral reportage.

fictional_entities: Dr. Ines Marlow, restoration ecologist (invented; all findings invented)
Rem Kessler, mesh-array engineer (invented; all quotes invented)
generator_meta.question_geometry: Q1 main idea (edge pos1) / Q2 detail / Q3 detail / Q4 inference / Q5 stance (edge pos5)
generator_meta.schema_note: Emits 5 questions per the long_passage_5q blueprint (ELF long passages are invariantly 5q). This exceeds candidate-item.schema.json questions.maxItems=4, which was authored for single-question seeded eval items; mech.py M-SCHEMA accepts 1-5. Intentional, flagged.
generator_meta.self_blind_solve: Solved each item from the passage alone before consulting the stored key: Q1->C, Q2->A, Q3->B, Q4->D, Q5->A (5/5). No two-way item survived.
